scenarioDescription: A large enterprise runs 500+ AWS accounts under AWS Organizations. Operations teams need to automatically detect non-compliant resources across all accounts, aggregate compliance findings centrally, and auto-remediate common violations like unencrypted S3 buckets and open security groups.
toolboxServices: AWS Config|AWS Systems Manager Automation|AWS CloudFormation StackSets|AWS Security Hub|Amazon Inspector|AWS CloudTrail|AWS Service Catalog
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Config|AWS Security Hub|AWS Systems Manager Automation
hint: A service that continuously evaluates resource configurations against rules is the starting point for compliance detection.
hint2: Security Hub aggregates findings from Config across all accounts into a single pane of glass with automated response actions.
explanation: AWS Config deploys managed and custom rules across all accounts via organization-wide Config to continuously evaluate resource compliance (unencrypted buckets, open security groups). AWS Security Hub aggregates Config compliance findings from all 500+ accounts into a centralized dashboard with cross-account visibility and automated finding workflows. AWS Systems Manager Automation runbooks are triggered by Security Hub custom actions or EventBridge rules to auto-remediate non-compliant resources — encrypting buckets, closing security groups — without manual intervention.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to patch 2,000 EC2 instances across 50 accounts on a weekly schedule with zero downtime. Instances must be patched in rolling waves (25% at a time), with automatic rollback if patch compliance drops below 95%. Patch compliance must be reported to a central operations account.
toolboxServices: AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager|AWS Systems Manager Maintenance Windows|AWS Systems Manager State Manager|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Organizations|AWS Health|Amazon CloudWatch
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager|AWS Systems Manager Maintenance Windows|Amazon CloudWatch|Amazon EventBridge
hint: Patch Manager defines patch baselines and scans instances for missing patches across multiple accounts.
hint2: Maintenance Windows orchestrate the rolling deployment with rate controls (25% concurrency) and error thresholds for automatic rollback.
explanation: AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager defines patch baselines specifying approved patches and scans all 2,000 instances for compliance across 50 accounts using delegated administrator. AWS Systems Manager Maintenance Windows schedule the patching in rolling waves with MaxConcurrency set to 25% and MaxErrors threshold triggering automatic rollback if compliance drops below 95%. Amazon CloudWatch collects patch compliance metrics, and Amazon EventBridge routes compliance state changes to the central operations account for aggregated reporting and alerting.

scenarioDescription: A financial institution experiences intermittent latency spikes in their multi-tier application. They need to trace requests end-to-end across API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB, and SQS to identify bottlenecks, correlate with infrastructure metrics, and create automated alarms when P99 latency exceeds thresholds.
toolboxServices: AWS X-Ray|Amazon CloudWatch|Amazon CloudWatch Synthetics|AWS CloudTrail|Amazon DevOps Guru|Amazon CloudWatch Application Insights|AWS Distro for OpenTelemetry
correctPipelineOrder: AWS X-Ray|Amazon CloudWatch Application Insights|Amazon CloudWatch
hint: Distributed tracing captures the full request path across all service boundaries to pinpoint where latency is introduced.
hint2: Application Insights automatically discovers application components and creates correlated metric dashboards and anomaly detection.
explanation: AWS X-Ray instruments the application to trace requests end-to-end across API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB, and SQS — generating service maps and identifying which component introduces latency spikes with segment-level timing data. Amazon CloudWatch Application Insights automatically discovers the application topology, correlates X-Ray traces with infrastructure metrics (CPU, memory, queue depth), and detects anomalies across the full stack. Amazon CloudWatch creates composite alarms on P99 latency metrics derived from X-Ray, triggering SNS notifications when thresholds are breached.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to enforce a standardized networking architecture across 200 AWS accounts. Every new account must automatically receive a VPC with specific CIDR ranges, transit gateway attachments, centralized DNS resolution, and firewall inspection — all without manual intervention from the networking team.
toolboxServices: AWS Control Tower|AWS CloudFormation StackSets|AWS Transit Gateway|AWS Network Firewall|Amazon Route 53 Resolver|AWS Service Catalog|AWS RAM (Resource Access Manager)
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Control Tower|AWS CloudFormation StackSets|AWS Transit Gateway|AWS Network Firewall|Amazon Route 53 Resolver
hint: Control Tower's lifecycle events trigger automated provisioning when new accounts are created or enrolled.
hint2: StackSets deploy standardized CloudFormation templates to new accounts automatically via Control Tower lifecycle hooks.
explanation: AWS Control Tower manages the multi-account environment and triggers lifecycle events when new accounts are created. AWS CloudFormation StackSets automatically deploy standardized VPC templates (specific CIDRs, subnets, route tables) to each new account via Control Tower lifecycle hooks. AWS Transit Gateway provides centralized connectivity with attachments shared via AWS RAM, AWS Network Firewall inspects all inter-VPC and egress traffic in a centralized inspection VPC, and Amazon Route 53 Resolver rules (shared via RAM) enable centralized DNS resolution across all 200 accounts.

scenarioDescription: A company's production Aurora cluster experienced an outage due to a misconfigured parameter group change. They need to prevent unauthorized infrastructure changes, detect configuration drift in real-time, automatically revert unauthorized modifications, and maintain an immutable audit trail of all changes.
toolboxServices: AWS Config|AWS CloudTrail|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Lambda|AWS Systems Manager Change Manager|AWS CloudFormation Drift Detection|AWS Backup
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Systems Manager Change Manager|AWS CloudTrail|AWS Config|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Lambda
hint: Change Manager enforces a formal change request and approval workflow before any infrastructure modifications are allowed.
hint2: Config rules detect when a resource configuration deviates from the approved state, triggering EventBridge events for automated remediation.
explanation: AWS Systems Manager Change Manager enforces a formal approval workflow — requiring change requests, approvals, and maintenance windows before any infrastructure modifications, preventing unauthorized parameter group changes. AWS CloudTrail provides the immutable audit trail of all API calls, while AWS Config continuously monitors resource configurations and detects drift from approved baselines in real-time. Amazon EventBridge captures Config compliance change events and triggers AWS Lambda functions that automatically revert unauthorized modifications back to the approved configuration state.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a cost optimization strategy across their organization. They need to identify idle and underutilized resources, rightsize EC2 instances based on actual usage, purchase Savings Plans for predictable workloads, and enforce tagging policies for cost allocation.
toolboxServices: AWS Cost Explorer|AWS Compute Optimizer|AWS Budgets|AWS Cost Anomaly Detection|AWS Trusted Advisor|AWS Organizations Tag Policies|Amazon CloudWatch
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Trusted Advisor|AWS Compute Optimizer|AWS Cost Explorer|AWS Organizations Tag Policies|AWS Budgets
hint: Trusted Advisor identifies idle resources (unused ELBs, unattached EBS volumes, idle RDS instances) as the first optimization step.
hint2: Compute Optimizer analyzes 14 days of CloudWatch metrics to recommend optimal instance types based on actual CPU, memory, and network utilization.
explanation: AWS Trusted Advisor identifies idle and underutilized resources (unused ELBs, unattached EBS volumes, idle RDS instances) for immediate cost savings. AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes CloudWatch utilization metrics to recommend rightsized EC2 instance types, EBS volumes, and Lambda memory configurations. AWS Cost Explorer analyzes historical spending patterns and recommends Savings Plans for predictable workloads. AWS Organizations Tag Policies enforce mandatory cost allocation tags across all accounts, and AWS Budgets creates spending alerts and automated actions when costs exceed thresholds.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a centralized logging architecture that collects VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail logs, and application logs from 100 accounts into a single data lake. Logs must be searchable within 5 minutes of generation, retained for 1 year in hot storage and 7 years in cold storage.
toolboxServices: Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose|Amazon S3|Amazon OpenSearch Service|AWS Lake Formation|Amazon CloudWatch Logs|S3 Glacier Deep Archive|AWS Glue
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon CloudWatch Logs|Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose|Amazon S3|Amazon OpenSearch Service|S3 Glacier Deep Archive
hint: CloudWatch Logs subscription filters can stream logs from multiple accounts to a centralized destination in near real-time.
hint2: Kinesis Data Firehose buffers and delivers logs to both S3 (data lake) and OpenSearch (search) simultaneously using multiple destinations.
explanation: Amazon CloudWatch Logs collects VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail logs, and application logs across all 100 accounts using cross-account log subscriptions. Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose receives the log streams via subscription filters and delivers them to both Amazon S3 (data lake for long-term retention) and Amazon OpenSearch Service (for sub-5-minute searchability) simultaneously. Amazon S3 stores logs in hot storage (S3 Standard/IA) for 1 year, then S3 lifecycle policies transition logs to S3 Glacier Deep Archive for the remaining 6 years of cold retention.

scenarioDescription: A company runs a critical application on EKS across three Availability Zones. They need to implement automated incident response that detects pod failures, node health issues, and resource exhaustion — automatically scaling, cordoning unhealthy nodes, and notifying the on-call team via PagerDuty within 2 minutes.
toolboxServices: Amazon CloudWatch Container Insights|Amazon EKS|AWS Fault Injection Simulator|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Lambda|AWS Systems Manager OpsCenter|Amazon SNS
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon CloudWatch Container Insights|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Lambda|Amazon SNS
hint: Container Insights collects cluster-level, node-level, and pod-level metrics and logs from EKS with pre-built dashboards.
hint2: EventBridge rules match specific metric alarm patterns and route them to Lambda for automated remediation before human notification.
explanation: Amazon CloudWatch Container Insights collects detailed EKS metrics (pod restarts, node CPU/memory, pending pods) and creates CloudWatch alarms for failure thresholds. Amazon EventBridge captures the alarm state changes and routes them based on severity — pod failures, node health degradation, and resource exhaustion each trigger different response paths. AWS Lambda executes automated remediation (scaling deployments, cordoning unhealthy nodes via kubectl, triggering Cluster Autoscaler) and Amazon SNS sends formatted notifications to PagerDuty within the 2-minute SLA.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a disaster recovery strategy for their multi-region application with an RTO of 5 minutes and RPO of 0. The application uses Aurora MySQL, ElastiCache Redis, and stores session data. Failover must be fully automated with no manual DNS changes.
toolboxServices: Amazon Aurora Global Database|Amazon ElastiCache Global Datastore|Amazon Route 53|AWS Global Accelerator|AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery|Amazon CloudFront|AWS Lambda
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon Aurora Global Database|Amazon ElastiCache Global Datastore|AWS Global Accelerator|Amazon Route 53
hint: Aurora Global Database provides cross-region replication with RPO of 0 using write forwarding and synchronous replication options.
hint2: Global Accelerator provides static anycast IPs with automatic health-check-based failover — faster than DNS TTL propagation.
explanation: Amazon Aurora Global Database replicates data synchronously to the secondary region achieving RPO of 0, with managed planned failover promoting the secondary cluster in under 1 minute. Amazon ElastiCache Global Datastore replicates Redis session data cross-region with sub-second lag, ensuring session continuity during failover. AWS Global Accelerator provides static anycast IP addresses with built-in health checks that automatically route traffic to the healthy region within seconds (faster than DNS TTL), while Amazon Route 53 health checks provide an additional failover layer for any region-specific endpoints.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement infrastructure as code governance. All infrastructure changes must go through a CI/CD pipeline with automated testing, security scanning, cost estimation, and approval gates before deployment. Manual console changes must be detected and reverted.
toolboxServices: AWS CloudFormation|AWS CodePipeline|AWS CodeBuild|cfn-nag|AWS Config|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Service Catalog
correctPipelineOrder: AWS CodePipeline|AWS CodeBuild|cfn-nag|AWS CloudFormation|AWS Config
hint: CodePipeline orchestrates the full CI/CD workflow with source, build, test, approval, and deploy stages.
hint2: cfn-nag performs static security analysis on CloudFormation templates before deployment, catching insecure configurations early.
explanation: AWS CodePipeline orchestrates the infrastructure CI/CD workflow — pulling templates from source control and managing stage transitions with manual approval gates. AWS CodeBuild runs automated tests including cfn-nag for security scanning (detecting open security groups, unencrypted resources), cost estimation, and template validation. AWS CloudFormation deploys the approved, scanned templates using change sets for safe infrastructure updates. AWS Config detects configuration drift from the CloudFormation-managed state, identifying any manual console changes for automated reversion.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to securely share VPC resources (subnets, transit gateway, Route 53 resolver rules) from a central networking account to 150 workload accounts without VPC peering. Resources must be shared automatically when new accounts join specific OUs, and sharing must be revocable.
toolboxServices: AWS RAM (Resource Access Manager)|AWS Organizations|AWS Transit Gateway|Amazon Route 53 Resolver|VPC Peering|AWS PrivateLink|AWS CloudFormation StackSets
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Organizations|AWS RAM (Resource Access Manager)|AWS Transit Gateway|Amazon Route 53 Resolver
hint: RAM integrates with Organizations to automatically share resources with entire OUs — new accounts inherit shares immediately.
hint2: Transit Gateway attachments shared via RAM allow workload accounts to connect their VPCs without managing individual peering connections.
explanation: AWS Organizations provides the OU structure that defines which accounts receive shared resources. AWS RAM (Resource Access Manager) shares Transit Gateway, subnets, and Route 53 Resolver rules from the networking account to specific OUs — new accounts joining those OUs automatically inherit the shared resources without manual intervention. AWS Transit Gateway provides hub-and-spoke connectivity for all 150 workload accounts through the shared attachment, and Amazon Route 53 Resolver rules (shared via RAM) enable centralized DNS resolution across the entire organization.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement automated security incident response. When GuardDuty detects a compromised EC2 instance (cryptocurrency mining), the system must automatically isolate the instance, capture forensic evidence (memory dump, disk snapshot), and create a ticket — all within 5 minutes.
toolboxServices: Amazon GuardDuty|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Lambda|AWS Step Functions|Amazon EC2|AWS Systems Manager|AWS Security Hub
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon GuardDuty|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Step Functions|AWS Lambda|AWS Systems Manager
hint: GuardDuty generates findings for cryptocurrency mining detection (CryptoCurrency:EC2/BitcoinTool.B) that trigger automated workflows.
hint2: Step Functions orchestrates the multi-step forensic workflow — isolation, evidence capture, and ticketing — with error handling and parallel execution.
explanation: Amazon GuardDuty detects the compromised instance via CryptoCurrency findings and publishes to Amazon EventBridge. EventBridge matches the finding type and triggers AWS Step Functions, which orchestrates the incident response workflow with parallel branches. AWS Lambda functions execute isolation (replacing the security group with a forensic isolation SG that blocks all traffic), while AWS Systems Manager Run Command captures memory dumps and creates EBS snapshots for forensic analysis — all completing within the 5-minute SLA.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a self-service infrastructure provisioning portal for development teams. Products must be pre-approved, version-controlled, and constrained to specific regions and instance types. Teams should only see products approved for their business unit, and all provisioned resources must be tagged automatically.
toolboxServices: AWS Service Catalog|AWS CloudFormation|AWS Organizations|AWS IAM|AWS Config|AWS Control Tower|AWS Proton
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Service Catalog|AWS CloudFormation|AWS Organizations|AWS Config
hint: Service Catalog provides a curated portfolio of pre-approved products that teams can self-service provision with launch constraints.
hint2: Launch constraints in Service Catalog execute CloudFormation with an admin role, so developers don't need direct infrastructure permissions.
explanation: AWS Service Catalog creates portfolios of pre-approved products (EC2 environments, RDS databases, EKS clusters) with launch constraints limiting regions, instance types, and enforcing mandatory tags — portfolios are shared to specific OUs so teams only see their approved products. AWS CloudFormation templates define the infrastructure with parameterized constraints, and Service Catalog's launch role executes deployments with elevated permissions so developers don't need direct IAM access. AWS Organizations enables portfolio sharing across OUs, and AWS Config validates that all provisioned resources maintain compliance with tagging and configuration rules.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement blue/green deployments for their Aurora PostgreSQL database during major schema migrations. The migration must support rollback capability, maintain read traffic during migration, and validate the new schema with production traffic before full cutover.
toolboxServices: Amazon Aurora Blue/Green Deployments|Amazon Aurora Read Replicas|Amazon RDS Proxy|AWS DMS|Amazon Route 53|AWS Lambda|Amazon CloudWatch
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon Aurora Blue/Green Deployments|Amazon RDS Proxy|Amazon CloudWatch|Amazon Route 53
hint: Aurora Blue/Green Deployments create a staging environment (green) that stays synchronized with production (blue) via logical replication.
hint2: RDS Proxy maintains persistent database connections, enabling seamless switchover between blue and green environments without application connection drops.
explanation: Amazon Aurora Blue/Green Deployments create a synchronized green environment where schema migrations are applied and tested while the blue production cluster continues serving traffic. Amazon RDS Proxy sits in front of both environments, maintaining connection pooling that enables seamless switchover without application-side connection string changes or drops. Amazon CloudWatch monitors key metrics (replication lag, query latency, error rates) on the green environment to validate the new schema under production traffic patterns. Amazon Route 53 weighted routing can gradually shift read traffic to validate before full cutover, with instant rollback by switching back to blue.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement chaos engineering practices to validate their application's resilience. They need to simulate AZ failures, inject CPU stress on specific EC2 instances, throttle DynamoDB tables, and measure the blast radius — all with automatic stop conditions to prevent production impact.
toolboxServices: AWS Fault Injection Simulator (FIS)|Amazon CloudWatch|AWS Systems Manager|Amazon EventBridge|AWS Resilience Hub|AWS Lambda|Amazon DevOps Guru
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Resilience Hub|AWS Fault Injection Simulator (FIS)|Amazon CloudWatch|AWS Systems Manager
hint: Resilience Hub first assesses your application's resiliency posture and identifies weaknesses before you inject faults.
hint2: FIS experiments include stop conditions tied to CloudWatch alarms that automatically halt the experiment if impact exceeds safe thresholds.
explanation: AWS Resilience Hub analyzes the application architecture, identifies single points of failure, and recommends resiliency improvements — establishing the baseline before chaos testing. AWS Fault Injection Simulator (FIS) runs controlled experiments — simulating AZ failures, injecting CPU stress via SSM Agent, and throttling DynamoDB — with stop conditions tied to CloudWatch alarms that automatically halt experiments if error rates or latency exceed safe thresholds. Amazon CloudWatch monitors the blast radius in real-time during experiments, and AWS Systems Manager executes the fault injection actions on target EC2 instances via SSM Agent.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a multi-account strategy where each business unit gets isolated accounts for dev, staging, and production. Accounts must be provisioned automatically with baseline security controls, networking, and logging — and decommissioned cleanly when no longer needed.
toolboxServices: AWS Control Tower|AWS Control Tower Account Factory|AWS Organizations|AWS CloudFormation StackSets|AWS Service Catalog|AWS SSO (IAM Identity Center)|AWS Config
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Control Tower|AWS Control Tower Account Factory|AWS CloudFormation StackSets|AWS SSO (IAM Identity Center)|AWS Config
hint: Control Tower provides the landing zone with pre-configured guardrails, logging, and security baselines for multi-account environments.
hint2: Account Factory (or Account Factory for Terraform) automates account provisioning with customizable blueprints for networking, security, and baseline configurations.
explanation: AWS Control Tower establishes the landing zone with mandatory guardrails (SCPs), centralized logging (CloudTrail, Config), and the organizational structure. AWS Control Tower Account Factory automates account provisioning with customizable blueprints that include VPC configurations, security baselines, and IAM roles for each environment (dev/staging/prod). AWS CloudFormation StackSets deploy additional standardized resources (monitoring agents, backup policies, compliance rules) across all accounts. AWS SSO (IAM Identity Center) provides federated access with permission sets mapped to OUs, and AWS Config ensures continuous compliance monitoring across all provisioned accounts.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a centralized secrets management strategy. Application secrets (database credentials, API keys) must be automatically rotated, accessed securely from ECS tasks and Lambda functions across 80 accounts, and audited for every access attempt.
toolboxServices: AWS Secrets Manager|AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store|AWS KMS|AWS CloudTrail|AWS RAM|AWS IAM|Amazon EventBridge
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Secrets Manager|AWS KMS|AWS CloudTrail|Amazon EventBridge
hint: Secrets Manager supports automatic rotation of database credentials using Lambda rotation functions on configurable schedules.
hint2: KMS customer-managed keys encrypt secrets at rest, and CloudTrail logs every GetSecretValue API call for complete access auditing.
explanation: AWS Secrets Manager stores and automatically rotates database credentials and API keys using built-in Lambda rotation functions on configurable schedules (30, 60, 90 days). AWS KMS customer-managed keys encrypt all secrets at rest with key policies controlling which cross-account roles can decrypt — ECS task roles and Lambda execution roles access secrets via resource-based policies or cross-account IAM roles. AWS CloudTrail logs every GetSecretValue and RotateSecret API call for complete audit trails, and Amazon EventBridge captures rotation failure events to trigger automated alerts and remediation.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement automated capacity planning for their Auto Scaling groups. They want to proactively scale based on predicted traffic patterns (daily peaks, weekly cycles) rather than reactively scaling on CPU thresholds, reducing both over-provisioning costs and under-provisioning latency.
toolboxServices: AWS Auto Scaling Predictive Scaling|Amazon CloudWatch|AWS Compute Optimizer|AWS Auto Scaling Target Tracking|Amazon Forecast|AWS Trusted Advisor|AWS Cost Explorer
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon CloudWatch|AWS Compute Optimizer|AWS Auto Scaling Predictive Scaling|AWS Auto Scaling Target Tracking
hint: Predictive Scaling uses machine learning to analyze 14 days of historical CloudWatch metrics and forecast future capacity needs.
hint2: Combining predictive scaling (proactive) with target tracking (reactive) provides both planned capacity and burst handling in a single policy.
explanation: Amazon CloudWatch collects the historical CPU, network, and ALB request metrics that feed the ML models. AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes utilization patterns to recommend optimal instance types and sizes before configuring scaling policies. AWS Auto Scaling Predictive Scaling uses ML to analyze 14 days of CloudWatch metric history, identifying daily and weekly traffic patterns to proactively pre-provision capacity before predicted peaks. AWS Auto Scaling Target Tracking provides the reactive safety net — handling unexpected traffic bursts that exceed predictions by scaling based on real-time CPU or request count thresholds.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a Well-Architected Review process across their organization. They want to automatically assess workloads against AWS best practices, track improvement items, prioritize high-risk findings, and measure architectural maturity over time across all business units.
toolboxServices: AWS Well-Architected Tool|AWS Trusted Advisor|AWS Security Hub|AWS Config|Amazon QuickSight|AWS Organizations|AWS Service Catalog
correctPipelineOrder: AWS Well-Architected Tool|AWS Trusted Advisor|AWS Config|Amazon QuickSight
hint: The Well-Architected Tool provides structured reviews against the six pillars with improvement plans and milestone tracking.
hint2: Trusted Advisor checks provide automated validation of specific Well-Architected best practices (security groups, S3 permissions, service limits).
explanation: AWS Well-Architected Tool conducts structured reviews of each workload against the six pillars (Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance, Cost, Sustainability), generating improvement plans with prioritized high-risk items and tracking milestones over time. AWS Trusted Advisor provides automated checks that validate specific best practices identified in the review — open security groups, S3 bucket permissions, service limit utilization. AWS Config provides continuous compliance data showing whether recommended changes have been implemented across accounts. Amazon QuickSight creates executive dashboards visualizing architectural maturity scores, improvement trends, and risk distribution across all business units.

scenarioDescription: A company needs to implement a centralized monitoring and observability platform. They need to collect metrics, logs, and traces from 300 accounts, create cross-account dashboards, implement composite alarms for complex failure scenarios, and enable AIOps for anomaly detection and root cause analysis.
toolboxServices: Amazon CloudWatch|Amazon CloudWatch Cross-Account Observability|Amazon DevOps Guru|AWS X-Ray|Amazon Managed Grafana|Amazon OpenSearch Service|AWS Systems Manager OpsCenter
correctPipelineOrder: Amazon CloudWatch Cross-Account Observability|Amazon CloudWatch|Amazon DevOps Guru|AWS Systems Manager OpsCenter
hint: Cross-Account Observability creates monitoring and source account links that share metrics, logs, and traces to a central monitoring account.
hint2: DevOps Guru uses ML to detect operational anomalies and correlate related findings into actionable insights with recommended remediation.
explanation: Amazon CloudWatch Cross-Account Observability establishes monitoring account links across all 300 accounts, enabling centralized access to metrics, logs, and traces without data duplication. Amazon CloudWatch creates cross-account dashboards with composite alarms that evaluate complex multi-metric, multi-account failure conditions (e.g., high latency AND increased errors AND queue depth growth). Amazon DevOps Guru applies ML-based anomaly detection across the aggregated operational data, automatically correlating related anomalies into insights with root cause analysis and remediation recommendations. AWS Systems Manager OpsCenter aggregates the findings into operational items (OpsItems) with runbook associations for structured incident management.
